summary: Jeff Atwood explores Richard Gabriel's influential 'Worse is Better' philosophy, arguing that simpler, pragmatic solutions consistently outcompete theoretically superior but complex alternatives. He illustrates this through the contrast between MIT's pursuit of 'the right thing' versus Unix's New Jersey approach favoring implementation simplicity. Using examples like x86 architecture outlasting Itanium, .config files versus the Windows registry, and the overcomplexity of COM and J2EE, Atwood demonstrates how simplicity wins in the real world. He concludes that developers should always err on the side of simplicity because simple solutions survive, prosper, and can actually be understood by people.
key_points: Implementation simplicity often matters more than interface simplicity - the Unix philosophy of returning error codes rather than implementing complex state management won out
Theoretically inferior but simpler technologies consistently outlast their 'better' competitors (x86 vs Itanium, .INI files vs registry)
Complexity is a survival disadvantage - the registry's theoretical superiority is undermined by its fragility and opacity
Programmers suffer from dangerous optimism about others' ability to handle complex solutions
The Strunk and White approach applies to code: keep removing complexity until you cannot remove any more
money_quotes: Worse-is-better, even in its strawman form, has better survival characteristics than the-right-thing.
x86 is widely regarded as total piece of crap. And it is. But it's a piece of crap honed to an incredibly sharp edge.
Anything with 'Enterprise' in the title, just substitute 'Complicated.'
Whenever possible, always err on the side of simplicity.
Why even write code at all when you can buy or steal open-source it?
Simple solutions survive and prosper because they work, and people can actually understand them.
Don't presume that everyone's smart enough to handle the fancy complex solution; optimism is a dangerous occupational hazard for programmers.
Like words on a page, when you cannot remove any more – you're done.
key_insight: Simple solutions consistently outcompete theoretically superior alternatives because they can actually be understood, maintained, and evolved by real humans in the real world.
